elements.sent-2411-001: The Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) is an innovative approach to solve a diverse array of partial differential equations (PDEs) leveraging the power of neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-002: This is achieved by minimizing the residual loss associated with the explicit physical information, usually coupled with data derived from initial and boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, a challenge arises in the context of nonlinear conservation laws where derivatives are undefined at shocks, leading to solutions that deviate from the true physical phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-004: To solve this issue, the physical solution must be extracted from the weak formulation of the PDE and is typically further bounded by entropy conditions.
elements.sent-2411-005: Within the numerical framework, finite volume methods (FVM) are employed to address conservation laws.
elements.sent-2411-006: These methods resolve the integral form of conservation laws and delineate the shock characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-007: Inspired by the principles underlying FVM, this paper introduces a novel Coupled Integrated PINN methodology that involves fitting the integral solutions of equations using additional neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-008: This technique not only augments the conventional PINN's capability in modeling shock waves, but also eliminates the need for spatial and temporal discretization.
elements.sent-2411-009: As such, it bypasses the complexities of numerical integration and reconstruction associated with non-convex fluxes.
elements.sent-2411-010: Finally, we show that the proposed new Integrated PINN performs well in conservative law and outperforms the vanilla PINN when tackle the challenging shock problems using examples of Burger's equation, Buckley-Leverett Equation and Euler System.
elements.sent-2411-011: In the evolving landscape of computational science, Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs)\cite{raissi2019, raissi2017 have emerged as an essential innovation in the field of machine learning for science, integrating development in Neural Networks (NNs) with the structured knowledge of physical laws, typically represented by Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), yields a powerful method to model inference and identification.}
elements.sent-2411-012: PDEs are fundamental tools in modeling and understanding complex phenomena across various fields, including physics, engineering, and finance.
elements.sent-2411-013: They describe how quantities evolve over space and time.
elements.sent-2411-014: Their versatility and applicability make PDEs essential for simulating real-world systems with spatially and temporally varying behavior.
elements.sent-2411-015: PINNs embed the physical equations as part of the loss function in neural network training, ensuring that solutions are not only data-adherent but also consistent with the underlying governing differential equations.
elements.sent-2411-016: This character addresses the critical challenge of respecting physical laws over traditional machine learning models that may deviate from physical realism and generalize poorly.
elements.sent-2411-017: Applying Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to problems with sudden changes or “discontinuities” has proven to be difficult and often underperforms compared to traditional numerical methods.
elements.sent-2411-018: Take, for example, a basic case like Burgers’ equation.
elements.sent-2411-019: If the diffusion term—a factor that smooths out changes—is set to zero, the solution becomes much more unstable and less accurate.
elements.sent-2411-020: Even when there’s only a single, fixed shock (a sharp change in value), the behavior of the model can vary dramatically.
elements.sent-2411-021: In the case of complex shocks, moving shocks, and multiple shocks, finding a stable and accurate solution becomes even harder\cite{diab2021pinnssolutionhyperbolicbuckleyleverett,deryck2022wpinns .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Another challenge is that solutions for PDEs usually depend heavily on previous time steps.
elements.sent-2411-023: PINNs often settle into an unchanged state where the solution “flattens” out when dealing with the conservation law, meaning it incorrectly remains constant instead of evolving over time.
elements.sent-2411-024: This problem leads to a huge error in the solution, as the network gets “stuck” in a simplified answer rather than accurately following the true dynamics of the equation, although the total loss is low.
elements.sent-2411-025: Previously, researchers have tried to counter the problems of low accuracy and stability using advanced training techniques and by respecting causality\cite{wang2022respecting,wang2023experts , especially for equations that change with time.}
elements.sent-2411-026: While these tweaks have shown improvement, they still don’t fully solve the issue addressed by shocks, particularly for conservation laws.
elements.sent-2411-027: When the flattening effect occurs, we notice that the training error doesn’t reach the desired low levels, suggesting the network is not fully capturing the behavior of the equation as it should and falls into a local minimum.
elements.sent-2411-028: Several studies \cite{mei2024unified ,\cite{jeon2021fvm ,\cite{PATEL2022110754 ,\cite{li2023finite ,\cite{li2024predicting preceding this work have explored the integration of Finite Volume Methods (FVMs) with Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, these approaches often rely on discretizing the spatio-temporal domain rather than maintaining continuity, with many requiring numerical integration during training.
elements.sent-2411-030: The primary challenges with the integration approach are (1) achieving accurate integration over the simulated solution \( u \) by numerical integration, and (2) respecting mathematical law in the process.
elements.sent-2411-031: Rajvanshi et al. \cite{rajvanshi2024integral similarly leverage neural networks to approximate integrals directly, focusing on obtaining integrals of \( u \) with respect to spatial dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Yet, when applied to shocks, differentiating these integrals becomes mathematically problematic due to discontinuities at shock locations, it is not rigorous to differentiate the integral of step function.
elements.sent-2411-033: Besides, we try to directly get the inference result from neural networks and avoid differentiation before the final result which may amplify errors due to minor fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-034: Other works have paired FVM with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), though they often lack precise modeling of shock behavior.
elements.sent-2411-035: Liu et al. \cite{liu2024discontinuity note that training points within discontinuity regions can lead to discrepancy and may increase training loss.}
elements.sent-2411-036: They introduce an approach that adjusts the weight of each training point based on its gradient, thus reducing the influence of points within shock regions.
elements.sent-2411-037: Deryck et al. \cite{deryck2022wpinns introduce weak-PINNs to address the weak form of PDEs, providing rigorous error bounds, while Chaumet et al. \cite{chaumet2023improving enhance weak-PINNs for greater accuracy and efficiency, also analyzing the limitations of classical PINNs with \( L^2 \) norms, although their results are facing challenges such as not accurate and damping, they can still provide ideas for future research.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Mao et al. \cite{MAO2020112789 improve accuracy by concentrating training samples around high-gradient regions, mitigating error propagation across the domain.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In this paper, we propose an algorithm that respects the mathematical rules by replacing the integral term of the forward solution with a DNN and using another neural network to solve the forward problem in order to avoid the problems of undefinedness and oscillations when differentiating.
elements.sent-2411-040: We tested our methods on the three most common equations and compared with the traditional numerical method and vanilla PINN and got the conclusion that our methods have the powerful generalized ability and high accuracy on conservation law, meanwhile, it maintains a similar performance on normal PDEs with vanilla PINNs.
elements.sent-2411-041: In section 3 we are going to present see the ability of Integrated-PINN in modeling classical forward problems and compare it with classical FVM and vanilla PINN.
elements.sent-2411-042: In section 4 we are going to see how the inverse problem performs under perfect condition and noisy conditions.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Preliminaries: Integral forms of conservation law
elements.sent-2411-043: For nonlinear conservative law, when the characteristic lines of different values intersect with each other, the shocks appear.\cite{LeVeque1992 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: The properties of the shock are determined by the weak form of the PDE(i.e. Rankine-Hugoniot jump condition).
elements.sent-2411-045: Original conservation law can be written as: \begin{equation}u_t+f(u)_x=0\end{equation} where $u$ is conserved quantity in the conservation law, $u_t$ denotes the partial derivative of $u$ with respect to time $t$, $f$ is is a flux function that describes how the quantity $u$ flows or moves through space, $f(t)_x$ is denotes the partial derivative of $f(u)$ with respect to space.
elements.sent-2411-046: For the weak form of conservation law, we multiply a test function $\phi: R \to R$ and integrate over space and time yield:\begin{equation}\int_{0}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} [\phi u_t+\phi f_x] \,dx\,dt =0\end{equation} Due to the PDE in conservation form, this equation must hold for any $\phi$ function.
elements.sent-2411-047: Therefore, by choosing the special test function $\phi$:\begin{equation}\phi(x,t) =  1 for $(x,t)\in[x_1,x_2]\times[t_1,t_2]$  0 for $(x,t)\notin[x_1-\epsilon,x_2+\epsilon]\times[t_1-\epsilon,t_2+\epsilon]$ \end{equation} and $\phi$ smooth in the intermediate strip of width $\epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-048: As $\epsilon$ approaches to 0, the weak form approaches to the integral form: \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dt}\int_{x_1}^{x_2} u(x,t)\,dx = f(u(x_1,t))-f(u(x_2,t))\end{equation} for all $x_1$, $x_2$ and t\cite{LeVeque1992 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: This integral form is what FVM solves.
elements.sent-2411-050: Since the integral form is satisfied for all $x_1$, $x_2$ and t, can we say it is equivalent to \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dt}\int u(x,t)\,dx = f(u(x,t))\end{equation} for all x, t in the space-time domain.
elements.sent-2411-051: This method can also be extended to high-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-052: Inspired by the integral form and corresponding FVM in numerical method, the Coupled Integral PINN (CI-PINN) uses 2 fully connected feedforward neural networks (DNN) as the core of the solver.
elements.sent-2411-053: The output of the first DNN1, $u(x,t)$ is used to approximate the solutions of PDE and the output of DNN2, $v(x,t)$ is used to approximate the indefinite integrals of solutions $u$ with respect to its space location.
elements.sent-2411-054: Following the rigorous form of conservation law, we will train the CI-PINN by the loss between true value and $u(x,t)$ on the points of boundary and initial state, which in agreement with the numerical PDE solvers, the physical loss, entropy loss among the interior points randomly located inside the spatial-time domain, and the integral loss to ensure the $u(x,t)$ and $v(x,t)$ have the correct relationship.
elements.sent-2411-055: Previously, scholars\cite{YUAN2022111260 had added an output neural in order to solve integro-differential equations (IDEs) and constrained the added output by adding a loss term for its automatic differentiation.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Borrowing that idea, to increase the power of the representation, we choose to use two neural networks for the solution.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{paper_1.jpg} \caption{Integrate PINN framework to solve the forward problem of nonlinear PDEs.} \label{fig:enter-label}
elements.sent-2411-057: Consistent with Figure 1, in order to make the output of the second neural network the result we want, we need to add a new output condition loss term: integration loss: \begin{equation}\mathrm{MSE}_\text{integral-loss}=\frac{1}{N_f}\sum_{i=1}^{N_f}[\frac{\partial}{\partial x} v(x_i,t_i) - u(x_i,t_i)]^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-058: Again, we do not require that the value of v be fixed, only that the differentiation of v with respect to x be restricted. \begin{equation}\mathrm{MSE}_\text{initial-boundary-loss}=\frac{1}{N_b}\sum_{j=1}^{N_b}[u_{IBC}(x_j,t_j) - u(x_j,t_j)]^2,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathrm{MSE}_\text{physical-loss}=\frac{1}{N_f}\sum_{i=1}^{N_f}[f(x_i,t_i) - v_t(x_i,t_i)]^2,\end{equation} where the boundary and initial loss only count from points on initial or boundary state and other losses are count from the whole time-space domain.
elements.sent-2411-059: The solution of the integral form of conservative law satisfies the Rankine-Hugoniot jump condition, but does not strictly guarantee the uniqueness of the solution, and mathematicians have allowed the solution of the integral form to converge to the physical solution by means of viscous vanishing\cite{LeVeque1992 , this is called entropy solution.}
elements.sent-2411-060: It has also been successfully demonstrated to be feasible on neural networks by adding entropy conditions\cite{PATEL2022110754 .}
elements.sent-2411-061:

abla_u \eta(u)^	op 
abla_u [F(u)] = 
abla_u q(u)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: We focus on forward problem with shock waves, which is well-documented limitation of vanilla PINN.
elements.sent-2411-064: In addition to comparing with PINN, we also use MUSCL (Monotone Upstream-Centered Schemes for Conservation Laws) scheme \cite{VANLEER1979101 as a benchmark to compare with our solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The MUSCL is a 2nd-order FVM used in the numerical solution of partial differential equations, particularly those involving fluid dynamics and other conservation laws.
elements.sent-2411-066: It was designed to achieve higher accuracy while preserving the conservation properties essential for solving hyperbolic conservation laws.
elements.sent-2411-067: The Burgers' equation is a fundamental partial differential equation classified as a convection–diffusion equation.
elements.sent-2411-068: It plays a significant role in several areas of applied mathematics, including fluid mechanics, gas dynamics, and traffic flow\cite{doi:10.1080/10618562.2010.523518}\cite{https://doi.org/10.1002/cpa.3160030302 .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Consider the following burgers' equation: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\end{equation} where \( u = u(x,t) \) represents the velocity field, \( t \) is time, \( x \) is the spatial coordinate, and \( \nu \) is the viscosity coefficient.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-070: When we deal with inviscid burgers' equation, \(\nu\)=0 and thus discontinuous may appear.
elements.sent-2411-071: We often encounter two kinds of discontinuities, due to the characteristic velocity of the burgers equation $f'(u)=u$, when the value of $u$ located on the left side is larger than the right side there will be a shock, and when the value of u on the left side is smaller than the value on the right side there will be a rarefaction\cite{LeVeque1992 , we can use the square wave as the initial condition to show both cases at once, where initial condition:$ u(x,0) = 1$ for $-0.5 < x < 0$ and 0 else where.}
elements.sent-2411-072: For all burgers equation, we deploy the MUSCL scheme discrete the space with length 2/256 and discrete time with step size 0.01s.
elements.sent-2411-073: Both CI-PINN and PINN are trained with 300 random sampling points on initial and boundary conditions and 30000 random sampling points inside the time-spatial domain with LBFGS optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-074: And we evaluated the error on the same points with discretization in MUSCL sheme.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{0.24\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height=2.5cm]{burger_exact.png} \caption{EXACT} \label{fig:subim1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.24\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height=2.5cm]{burger_ipinn.png} \caption{CI-PINN} \label{fig:subim2} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.24\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height=2.5cm]{burger_pinn.png} \caption{PINN} \label{fig:subim2} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.24\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height=2.5cm]{burger_muscl.png} \caption{MUSCL} \label{fig:subim2} \end{subfigure} \caption{Contours of different methods} \begin{subfigure}{\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height=3.5cm]{burgerclip.png} \end{subfigure} \caption{Solution clips of different methods} \label{fig:image2}
elements.sent-2411-075: Figure 2 and 3 show the result of the forward solution comparing CI-PINN with vanilla PINN and the numerical method of MUSCL.
elements.sent-2411-076: Qualitatively, our method is competitive with traditional numerical methods yet PINN fails to capture the shock wave that occurred.
elements.sent-2411-077: On the quantitative comparison, Table 1 shows CI-PINN achieved similar accuracy as the classical numerical solver, while vanilla PINN have a larger error, see Table 1.
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{tabular}{ |p{3cm}|p{3cm}|p{3cm}| } method MAE error MSE error MUSCL scheme 9.295279e-03 1.254356e-03 CI-PINN 9.012409e-03 3.131935e-03 PINN 5.683641e-02 1.766200e-02 \end{tabular} \caption{Error compared with true solution}
elements.sent-2411-078: In the viscous scenario with diffusion term, for the burgers equation with a diffusion term, both PINN and CI-PINN perform well.
elements.sent-2411-080: The displacement of two immiscible fluids represents a prevalent challenge in the study of fluid flow within porous media.
elements.sent-2411-081: This phenomenon is typically formulated as a PDE, known as the Buckley-Leverett (B-L) problem \cite{muskat1938flow .}
elements.sent-2411-082: This formulation provides a mathematical framework to explore the dynamics and interactions of these fluids under varying conditions in porous substrates, for example, it is a key model in secondary oil recovery, where water is injected into underground rock formations to displace additional unrecovered oil.
elements.sent-2411-083: Below shows the reference solution, prediction of vanilla PINN and CI-PINN with initial condition:\begin{equation}u(x,0) =  1, if  -1 \leq x \leq -0.5, 0, if  -0.5 < x \leq 1. \end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-084: On the quantitative comparison, Table 2 shows that CI-PINN performs better than MUSCL while PINN has a large error.
elements.sent-2411-085: From the contour plot, we can see while PINN successfully simulates the continuous area, the shock wave behaves unreasonably.
elements.sent-2411-086: In Figure 5 at time t=0.5, the PINN notably missed a shock around x=0.5 and incorrectly smoothed it out, while CI-PINN captured the shock reasonably well.
elements.sent-2411-087: Moreover, at time t=1, the shock is led by a smooth descent, which CI-PINN again correctly captured.
elements.sent-2411-088: In the example of B-L equation, another advantage of ci-pinn is demonstrated, that is ci-pinn not only completely respects the math, but it also doesn't differentiate at the breakpoints and sharp point.
elements.sent-2411-089: At the same time, due to the nature of the weak solution, there is no need of the construction of convex hall for fluxes which have S shape curve like in the previous works\cite{diab2021pinnssolutionhyperbolicbuckleyleverett,fraces2021physics .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Consistent with various analyses, PINN still has fairly accurate predictive power when the solution is in a smooth region with rarefaction, and when it encounters a shock, PINN performance deteriorates and affects the solution in other space-time location.
elements.sent-2411-091: The Euler equations of gas dynamics constitute a system of hyperbolic partial differential equations governing the motion of an inviscid (non-viscous) fluid.
elements.sent-2411-092: These equations are derived from the fundamental principles of conservation of mass, momentum, and energy, and are widely employed in the fields of fluid dynamics and aerodynamics to model the behavior of gases\cite{toro2013riemann .}
elements.sent-2411-093: It is often used to judge the accuracy of numerical methods.. Euler system: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial U}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot F = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-094: Since vanilla PINN is not good at solving shocks, the following section will not compare the PINN solution to the other methods.
elements.sent-2411-095: See supporting information Figure~\ref{fig:vanilla12 for justification.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Thus, we will only compare the difference between CI-PINN, MUSCL, and the exact solution.

elements.sent-2411-100: The Sod shock tube problem is a classic test case in computational fluid dynamics, used to evaluate the accuracy of numerical solvers in capturing shocks, contact discontinuities, and rarefaction waves.
elements.sent-2411-101: The problem involves an ideal gas in a one-dimensional tube, initially divided by a diaphragm.
elements.sent-2411-102: The left side contains gas at high density and pressure, while the right side has lower density and pressure.
elements.sent-2411-103: Both sides are at rest initially\cite{SOD19781 .When the diaphragm is removed, the system evolves according to the Euler equations, producing different characteristic waves.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Consider the initial condition as below:\begin{equation}(\rho, u, p) =  (3, 0, 3), if  0 \leq x \leq 0.5, (1, 0, 1), if  0.5 < x \leq 1. \end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}(\rho, u, p) =  (3, 0, 3), if  0 \leq x \leq 0.5, (1, 0, 1), if  0.5 < x \leq 1. \end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-106: CI-PINN, as Figure 7 shows, performs satisfactorily, close to the gold-standard accuracy of the fine-grained numerical solver of MUSCL.
elements.sent-2411-107: At the same time, CI-PINN correctly simulates all the shockwave and rarefaction movements, which reaches the satisfactory accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-108: The Lax problem is a type of Riemann problem characterized by the presence of a strong shock and a pronounced contact discontinuity which is proven to be a complicated question for most of the existing NN methods due to the completely different shape with initial condition.
elements.sent-2411-109: The initial conditions for this problem are specified as follows:\begin{equation}(\rho, u, p) =  (0.445, 0.698, 3.528), if  0 \leq x \leq 0.5, (0.5, 0, 0.571), if  0.5 < x \leq 1. \end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-110: MUSCL scheme and sampling methods for training points are same as sod problems.
elements.sent-2411-111: And as shown in \cite{PATEL2022110754 , due to the inherent bias of neural networks towards continuous and smooth approximations, the NN that simulates weak form can be affected as shock strength increases, that is because, after the initial condition the solution of $\rho$ and $u$ change abruptly just in the next moment, this rapid changes will not fully captured by CI-PINN thus lead to the further error in the later time location, so we can see in the Lax problem, the neural networks cannot inference an accurate solution, especially for the most complex component $\rho$, the neural network completely lost the shock on the left side.}
elements.sent-2411-112: How to improve the ability of neural networks in the face of strong shocks has also become the next research direction.
elements.sent-2411-113: We have considered different forms of integration, such as integrating the original PDE twice: \begin{equation}\int u(x,t)\,dx = \int f(u(x,t))\,dt\end{equation} This form is more concise in terms of the loss term of the neural network, but it faces two problems at the same time, one is that we get the result as an integral with respect to the solution u, and even if his optimization achieves a better accuracy, we have to face the case of auto-differentiating again when we want to get the solution, which increases the error and damping of the solution.
elements.sent-2411-114: Secondly, consider a step function whose integral at the discontinuity point cannot be defined by differentiation because the left derivative is not consistent with the right one.
elements.sent-2411-115: For the method of integration, it is natural to think about neural operator, s.t deeponet\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1910-03193 which learns the mapping from function to function.}
elements.sent-2411-116: However, In practice, their speed and accuracy are not as good as directly deploying DNN and applying appropriate constraints, resulting in poor performance of CI-PINN using deeponet as integrator.
elements.sent-2411-117: I believe this is because deeponet itself is not suitable for processing non-continuous functions.
elements.sent-2411-118: Regarding future directions, significant efforts over the past decades have been devoted to the analysis and development of the Finite Element Method (FEM) \cite{brenner2008mathematical, thomee2007galerkin .}
elements.sent-2411-119: FEM excels in handling complex geometries and has enhanced the robustness of numerical solutions.
elements.sent-2411-120: The utilization of finite element spaces is emerging as the next frontier for neural networks (NNs) in solving partial differential equations (PDEs), both for forward and inverse problems, the success of coupled neural networks provides us a feasible approach to combine NNs with FEM and thus to improve the stability and generalizability of solutions.
elements.sent-2411-121: In parallel, alternative statistical approaches have achieved success in simulation and inference.
elements.sent-2411-122: Researchers have developed highly effective methods that combine Gaussian processes with Bayesian inference \cite{doi:10.1073/pnas.2020397118, doi:10.1137/22M1514131 .}
elements.sent-2411-123: These methods offer robust frameworks for dealing with uncertainties and improving predictive capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-124: Moreover, enhancing the generalization of algorithms remains a critical area that warrants further investigation.
elements.sent-2411-125: Advancements in this direction are essential for developing more universally applicable models that perform reliably across a wide range of scenarios.
